description.text: Augment your digital engagement strategy with online and offline events

body:0001.text: What are events on the platform?

body:0002.text: Events on the platform let you blend offline, online, and digital engagement into your strategy.

body:0003.text: Whether it’s a public meeting at City Hall, an online workshop, or a pop-up event at a metro station, the Events feature lets you display, manage, and publicize past and upcoming events all in one place.

body:0005.text: Creates more touchpoints for your community

body:0006.text: Makes it easy to manage registrations and track attendees directly on the platform

body:0007.text: Bridges online participation with real‑world engagement

body:0008.text: How are events displayed on the platform?

body:0010.text: All events from across projects are listed on one page.

body:0011.text: Access by adding /events to your platform URL.

body:0012.text: Or add “Events” to the navigation bar via Pages & Menu → Add to navbar.

body:0014.text: Events show at the bottom of the project page. If they’re linked to a phase, they also appear in the phase description.

body:0016.text: Standard & Premium plans can show up to three upcoming events via the Events Widget.

body:0017.text: Enable via Pages & Menu → Edit (Home) → update the “Events” section.

body:0018.text: How to set up an event?

body:0020.text: Open the project where you want the event.

body:0023.text: Fill in event title, description, image, and attachments

body:0024.text: Set the event date and time (residents and project managers see times converted to their own browser timezone, with a timezone abbreviation appended)

body:0025.text: Add location details (including hybrid or fully online events)

body:0026.text: Optional: enable registration limit and set maximum allowed registrations

body:0028.text: If the event is a single day, you can create a one‑day‑long phase for scheduling flexibility.

body:0029.text: How to remove the registration button from an event?

body:0030.text: You can turn off the register button for events. Here's how:

body:0031.text: 1. Go to the project where the event is attached.

body:0032.text: 2. Navigate to the Events tab and click Edit.

body:0034.text: 4. Switch it to Link the button to an external URL

body:0035.text: This action will change the attendance button's function, effectively turning off the registration for that event. Instead of allowing users to register, you can make it an information button by linking it to the event page and calling the button “More Information”. Remember, this method doesn't completely remove the button, but it does prevent users from registering through it.

body:0037.text: Residents can register up until the event ends (e.g., if the event runs until 19:00, registration is open until 19:00).

body:0038.text: Set up access rights for your event

body:0039.text: The access rights for your event are linked to the phase of the project that is currently active. To read more about access rights read this support article.

body:0041.text: If access rights play an important role in your event, we recommend creating a dedicated project for it. Configure a single phase (for example, an Information phase) that runs from the current date until the event date.

body:0042.text: This ensures that the access rights defined for this phase remain consistent throughout the entire registration process.

body:0044.text: Turn on Registration limit toggle in event settings.

body:0045.text: Enter max registrations. Users will see how many spots are left.

body:0046.text: How to manage event attendees and communication?

body:0047.text: You can export attendees via the Events tab. Attendees automatically get a confirmation email after signing up.

body:0049.text: Creating a smart group of attendees.

body:0050.text: Using the Messaging tab to send emails (before the event with prep materials or after the event for follow‑up engagement).

body:0052.text: Ways local governments are successfully using events:

body:0053.text: Kick-off events: Launch a project and create buzz.

body:0054.text: Relationship & trust-building events: Drop-in meet-and-greets with council members. (Example: Municipality of Houten engaged 345 attendees.)

body:0055.text: Information-sharing events: Topic‑specific meetings before major projects. (Example: City of Vienna for local planning.)

body:0056.text: Project-site events: Gather feedback directly at project locations (e.g., playground design discussions).

body:0057.text: Reveal events: Celebrate milestones or unveil final results. (Example: City of Ostend’s Christmas lights project.)

body:0058.text: Pop-up events: Surprise events to reach new audiences. (Example: Copenhagen pop-up at a metro station.)

body:0059.text: Educational workshops: Teach residents about city operations or policies. (Examples: Chile on gender lens; Netherlands on wind turbines.)

body:0060.text: Offline voting events: Combine in-person and online voting (offline votes can be uploaded to the platform).
